content.title: 2011 ASA/ACCF/AHA/AANN/AANS/ACR/ASNR/CNS/SAIP/SCAI/SIR/SNIS/SVM/SVS Guideline on the Management of Patients With Extracranial Carotid and Vertebral Artery Disease
content.purpose: A report by various associations including the American College of Cardiology Foundation, American Heart Association, American Stroke Association, and others to provide guidelines on managing patients with extracranial carotid and vertebral artery disease.
content.acknowledgments: The committee acknowledges the memory of Robert W. Hobson II, MD. Document approval was by multiple authorities including the American College of Cardiology Foundation and the American Heart Association Science Advisory and Coordinating Committee. The guideline was accepted in November 2010.

content.preamble: The American College of Cardiology Foundation (ACCF) and the American Heart Association (AHA) have jointly engaged in the production of guidelines in the area of cardiovascular disease since 1980. The ACCF/AHA Task Force on Practice Guidelines (Task Force) is charged with developing, updating, and revising practice guidelines for cardiovascular diseases and procedures. Writing committees perform a formal literature review and weigh the strength of evidence for recommendations.

content.Title: Applying Classification of Recommendations and Level of Evidence

Class I.Definition: Benefit >>> Risk. Procedure/Treatment SHOULD be performed/administered.
Class I.Level A: Multiple populations evaluated. Data derived from multiple randomized clinical trials or meta-analyses.
Class I.Level B: Limited populations evaluated. Data derived from a single randomized trial or nonrandomized studies.
Class I.Level C: Very limited populations evaluated. Only consensus opinion of experts, case studies, or standard of care.

Class IIa.Definition: Benefit >> Risk. Additional studies with focused objectives needed. IT IS REASONABLE to perform procedure/administer treatment.
Class IIa.Level A: Multiple populations evaluated. Data derived from multiple randomized clinical trials or meta-analyses.
Class IIa.Level B: Limited populations evaluated. Data derived from a single randomized trial or nonrandomized studies.
Class IIa.Level C: Very limited populations evaluated. Only consensus opinion of experts, case studies, or standard of care.

Class IIb.Definition: Benefit ≥ Risk. Additional studies with broad objectives needed. Additional registry data would be helpful. Procedure/Treatment MAY BE CONSIDERED.
Class IIb.Level A: Recommendation’s usefulness/effectiveness is less well established. Greater conflicting evidence from multiple randomized trials or meta-analyses.
Class IIb.Level B: Recommendation’s usefulness/effectiveness is less well established. Greater conflicting evidence from single randomized trial or nonrandomized studies.
Class IIb.Level C: Recommendation’s usefulness/effectiveness is less well established. Only diverging expert opinion, case studies, or standard of care.

No Benefit.Definition: Procedure/Treatment is not useful/effective and may be harmful.
No Benefit.Level A: Sufficient evidence from multiple randomized trials or meta-analyses.
No Benefit.Level B: Recommendation that procedure or treatment is not useful/effective and may be harmful.
No Benefit.Level C: Recommendation that procedure or treatment is not useful/effective and may be harmful.

Harm.Definition: Procedure/Treatment is not recommended and may be harmful.
Harm.Level A: Causes harm according to multiple randomized trials or meta-analyses.
Harm.Level B: Causes harm according to single randomized trial or nonrandomized studies.
Harm.Level C: Causes harm based on expert opinion, case studies, or standard of care.

Comparator Phrases.Class IIa, Level of Evidence A or B: Comparative effectiveness phrases have been suggested.

Task Force Information.Purpose: To avoid actual, potential, or perceived conflicts of interest.
Task Force Information.Writing Committee: The writing committee members volunteered their time.

content.introduction: The ACCF/AHA guidelines are intended to assist healthcare providers in clinical decision making by providing generally acceptable approaches for diagnosis, management, and prevention of specific diseases or conditions. The guidelines are based on a thorough review of scientific evidence and aim to improve patient care by defining practices that meet most patients' needs in most circumstances. Clinical judgment and local care availability should be considered in applying these guidelines, with the overarching goal of improving care quality.
content.methodology_and_evidence_review: The ACCF/AHA writing committee developed the 2011 guidelines for managing patients with Extracranial Carotid and Vertebral Artery Disease (ECVD) through comprehensive literature reviews, focusing on various aspects of carotid and vertebral artery intervention as of May 2010. Searches were limited to English-language publications and addressed conditions such as atherosclerosis, carotid artery disease, and stroke, among others.
content.evidence_based_methodologies: The Task Force utilized evidence-based methodologies, although adjustment for different interventions posed challenges. The document focuses on vascular diseases themselves and not on managing patients with acute stroke or prevention of disease across a population, which is covered under a different guideline.

definitions.definition: A frequent variant where the brachiocephalic and left common carotid arteries share a common origin.

Figure 1 Explanation.Panel A: The most common aortic arch branching pattern in humans has separate origins for the innominate, left common carotid, and left subclavian arteries.
Figure 1 Explanation.Panel B: A pattern with a common origin for the innominate and left common carotid arteries, sometimes erroneously referred to as a 'bovine arch.'
Figure 1 Explanation.Panel C: A variant where the left common carotid artery originates separately from the innominate artery, also mistakenly called a 'bovine arch.'
Figure 1 Explanation.Panel D: A branching pattern found in cattle with a single brachiocephalic trunk eventually splitting into bilateral subclavian arteries and a bicarotid trunk.

Cerebrovascular Disease and Stroke.Risks: Patients with extracranial cerebrovascular disease (ECVD) have increased risks for other cardiovascular events like myocardial infarction (MI), peripheral arterial disease (PAD), and death.

content.Epidemiology: Stroke is a leading cause of death in industrialized nations, ranking third after heart disease and cancer.

general_information: The prevalence of stroke varies regionally, and geographic data may not be broadly applicable.
The Great Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky Stroke Study reported approximately 700,000 stroke events annually.
Increased stroke prevalence found in the southeastern 'stroke belt' of the U.S.
Disparities in stroke incidence are noted among different racial/ethnic groups, with higher occurrences in Black and Hispanic populations compared to Whites.
Evidence of silent cerebral infarction increases with age, affecting a significant percentage of older adults.
Approximately 54% of stroke deaths in 1999 were due to unspecified causes.
Extracranial carotid stenosis is more prevalent in men and increases with age.
The prevalence of ≥50% carotid stenosis was 7% in men and 5% in women aged 66 to 93.
Carotid plaque was more frequently found in older populations.
Subclinical carotid disease is associated with increased stroke risk over long periods.
Atherosclerotic disease of extracranial carotid and vertebral arteries is similar to other arteriopathies, beginning with lipid accumulation.

content.classI: In asymptomatic patients with known or suspected carotid stenosis, duplex ultrasonography by a qualified technologist in a certified laboratory is the initial diagnostic test recommended to detect hemodynamically significant carotid stenosis.
classIIa: It is reasonable to perform duplex ultrasonography to detect hemodynamically significant carotid stenosis in asymptomatic patients with carotid bruit.

It is also reasonable to repeat duplex ultrasonography annually by a qualified technologist to assess the progression or regression of disease and response to therapeutic interventions in patients with atherosclerosis who have had stenosis greater than 50% detected previously.
classIIb: Duplex ultrasonography to detect hemodynamically significant carotid stenosis may be considered in asymptomatic patients with symptomatic PAD, coronary artery disease, aortic aneurysm, but its clinical outcomes are unclear.

It might be considered to detect carotid stenosis in asymptomatic patients without clinical evidence of atherosclerosis but with risk factors like hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a family history of ischemic stroke. However, outcomes are uncertain.
classIIInobenefit: Carotid duplex ultrasonography is not recommended for screening of asymptomatic patients who have no clinical manifestations or risks for atherosclerosis.
It is not recommended for routine evaluation of patients with unrelated neurological or psychiatric disorders.
Routine serial imaging of the extracranial carotid arteries is not recommended for patients without risk factors for atherosclerotic carotid disease.

Recommendations.Screening: The AHA/ASA guideline for stroke prevention recommended against screening the general population for asymptomatic carotid stenosis due to lack of cost-effectiveness, false positives, and the small benefit of intervention.
Recommendations.Target Group: The American Society of Neuroimaging suggested screening adults older than 65 with three or more cardiovascular risk factors. The ACCF/SCAI/SVMB/SIR/ASITN panel recommended screening symptomatic patients with carotid bruits and candidates for coronary artery bypass.

content.Extracranial Cerebrovascular Disease: Patients with extracranial carotid or vertebral atherosclerosis often have systemic atherosclerosis. They face increased risks of MI and death, especially those with carotid stenosis, EVCVD, and coronary atherosclerosis.
content.Carotid IMT: IMT measurement is an indicator of systemic atherosclerosis, although not yet a recognized screening method for atherosclerotic risk in the US.

Clinical Presentation.3.1 Summary: Extracranial atherosclerotic disease accounts for 15% to 20% of ischemic strokes. The relationship between plaque severity and ischemic events is complex. Medical therapy's effect on stroke risk varies with the degree of stenosis. The Framingham Heart Study showed a higher stroke incidence in patients with cervical bruits. Studies indicated the natural history involves various stroke risk scenarios.
Clinical Presentation.3.2 Summary: Severity of stenosis correlates with different parameters of plaque vulnerability and stroke risk. Characteristics like ulceration and echolucency are key in assessing stenosis severity. Imaging methods, including MRI and PET, are used for characterizing lesions and evaluating metabolic activity in plaques.

sections.content: Macrophage activity quantified by PET and neovascular angiogenesis assessed by MRI have been observed in experimental models. Biomarkers such as C-reactive protein and certain matrix metalloproteinases with the potential to identify carotid plaque instability have also been investigated, but the reliability of biomarkers in predicting clinical events has not been established. Several studies have shown that plaque composition is modified by treatment with statins. Despite these advances in understanding the pathophysiology of atherosclerotic plaque, the utility of biomarkers in predicting clinical events remains uncertain.

3.3 Symptoms and Signs of Transient Isch.TIA Definition: TIA is defined as a syndrome of acute neurological dysfunction referable to a single brain artery, characterized by symptoms lasting <24 hours.
3.3 Symptoms and Signs of Transient Isch.Revised Definition: A revised definition exists for symptoms that last <1 hour, although the typical duration is <15 minutes.
3.3 Symptoms and Signs of Transient Isch.Stroke Symptoms: Include left-sided weakness, paresthesia, apraxia, ataxia, dizziness, imbalance, etc.
3.3 Symptoms and Signs of Transient Isch.Prehospital Delay: Factors include clinical characteristics and demographic, cognitive, and behavioral factors.

4 Clinical Assessment of Patients With F.4.1 Acute Ischemic Stroke: Guidelines for emergency stroke care should be followed. Thrombolytic therapy administered, and preventive measures taken.
4 Clinical Assessment of Patients With F.4.2 Transient Ischemic Attack: TIA is important for identifying stroke risk. Approximately 240,000 TIAs diagnosed annually in the US. Early recognition critical.
4 Clinical Assessment of Patients With F.4.3 Amaurosis Fugax: Caused by temporary reduction of blood flow to an eye resulting in monocular blindness.

content.Public Awareness: Materials developed by AHA and ASA focus on recognizing symptoms of TIA and stroke and encouraging seeking immediate care. Initiatives led to improvement in public knowledge.

content.vision_loss: Transient monocular blindness is often associated with ischemic events and may lead from carotid artery stenosis or occlusion, among other causes.
content.stroke_risk: Stroke risk is lower in patients with transient monocular blindness than those with hemispheric TIA. Medical treatment alone can reduce the stroke risk associated with transient monocular blindness, which is related to the number of stroke risk factors such as hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, diabetes, and smoking.
content.intracranial_ischemia: Caused by atherosclerosis, vasculitis, or tumors etc. Intracranial evaluation may be crucial for presenting high-grade lesions.
content.atherosclerotic_disease: Significant as a cerebral ischemia risk factor, though specific guidelines for aortic arch disease need to be referred to.
content.neurological_symptoms: Nonfocal neurological symptoms in TIA patients may include transient global amnesia and nonrotational dizziness, highlighting the complexity in diagnosis.
content.diagnostic_testing: For patients with neurological symptoms, initial evaluations should consider possible ischemic origins with noninvasive imaging methods like duplex ultrasonography recommended.

Level of Evidence.C: Recommendation supported by expert opinion, case studies, or standard of care.

Imaging and Diagnostic Recommendations: Noninvasive imaging is recommended for initial evaluation of carotid stenosis.
MRA or CTA is suggested when ultrasound is equivocal.
Carotid ultrasound may be considered in patients with retinal or hemispheric neurological symptoms with suspected ischemic origin.
CT or MRI may be used for better-quality imaging.
Therapeutic Recommendations: Catheter-based angiography may be needed for definitive diagnosis or to assess lesion severity when noninvasive results are inconclusive.
Catheter-based angiography may be an option in patients with renal dysfunction.
Indications for Imaging: Vascular assessment in patients with multiple risk factors for atherosclerosis.
Stroke risk assessment for CAD or PAD patients.
Special Considerations: Importance of consistent imaging assessment to ensure high quality.
Exploration of the accuracy of noninvasive imaging techniques compared with catheter-based angiography.

content.summary: The document discusses the assessment of arterial stenosis severity for surgical or endovascular intervention, particularly focusing on the use of carotid duplex ultrasonography. This technique evaluates the internal carotid artery by measuring peak systolic velocity to determine stenosis. The document emphasizes the complexity of distinguishing between various stenosis severities and the importance of corroborating results with angiographic measurements. It outlines that a peak systolic velocity of 125 to 230 cm/s in the internal carotid artery indicates a stenosis between 50% to 69%. Furthermore, stenosis above 70% is associated with higher velocities above 230 cm/s. The text also highlights challenges in ultrasonic measurements due to patient-specific factors and suggests methods to improve diagnostic accuracy.
key_points: Carotid duplex ultrasonography is used for assessing arterial stenosis.
A peak systolic velocity of 125 to 230 cm/s is indicative of 50% to 69% stenosis.
Velocities above 230 cm/s typically indicate >70% stenosis.
Challenges include variability in patients and effects of calcification.
Accuracy is dependent on the expertise of technologists and calibration of imaging systems.

Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA).Description: MRA can generate high-resolution noninvasive images of the cervical arteries. It helps in detailed evaluation of the cerebral arteries. Slowly flowing blood is better imaged with contrast-enhanced MRA.
Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA).Technological advances: Has reduced image acquisition time and improved the quality of MRA, allowing for better evaluation of patients with ECVD.

Duplex Ultrasonography.Sensitivity and Specificity: Varies based on degree of stenosis, with sensitivity ranging from around 75% to 100% and specificity from around 69% to 100%.

Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA).Pitfalls: Overestimation of stenosis, inability to distinguish subtotal and complete occlusion, issues with claustrophobia and compatibility with implanted devices.
Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA).Adverse Reactions: Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis in patients with renal dysfunction when using gadolinium-based contrast agents.

Computed Tomographic Angiography (CTA).Capabilities: Provides anatomic imaging and evaluation of tortuous vessels; facilitates evaluation of occlusion.
Computed Tomographic Angiography (CTA).Technological Evolution: Improved resolution and faster imaging with newer detectors.
Computed Tomographic Angiography (CTA).Limitations: Accuracy affected by higher degrees of stenosis and smaller vessel diameters.

content.Title: Sensitivity and Specificity of Computed Tomographic Angiography as a Function of Degree of Carotid Stenosis
content.Summary: The table contains data on the sensitivity and specificity of computed tomographic angiography (CTA) for varying degrees of carotid stenosis. Studies indicate that CTA is comparable to catheter angiography with high sensitivity and specificity, particularly for <70% carotid artery stenosis (100% specificity). The document discusses the advantages and limitations of CTA, such as the need for iodinated contrast media and potential issues with assessing heavily calcified lesions.
content.Section 5.5: Catheter-based angiography remains the standard for evaluating ECVD. There are challenges, including the risk of stroke associated with the procedure, which is estimated to be <1%.

content.Section: Medical Therapy for Patients With Atherosclerotic Disease of the Extracranial Carotid or Vertebral Arteries
content.Details: Carotid duplex ultrasonography is favored for screening patients at moderate risk. If results are equivocal, MRA or CTA may be used to explore disease extent. The method's choice depends on individual patient conditions such as renal insufficiency or metal implants.

Recommendations.Class I: Antihypertensive treatment is strongly recommended to maintain blood pressure below 140/90 mm Hg.
Recommendations.Class IIa: During hyperacute periods, antihypertensive treatment may be considered but without a specific target blood pressure.
Considerations: The risk of stroke increases with higher blood pressure.
Approximately 30%-45% increase in stroke risk per 10-mm Hg increase in systolic pressure.

Cessation of Tobacco Smoking.Recommendation: Patients with extracranial carotid or vertebral atherosclerosis who smoke cigarettes should be advised to quit smoking and offered smoking cessation interventions to reduce the risks of atherosclerosis progression and stroke.

Recommendations.Class I: Treatment with a statin medication is recommended for all patients with extracranial carotid or vertebral atherosclerosis to reduce low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol below 100 mg/dL.

Class IIa: Treatment with a statin medication is reasonable for all patients with extracranial carotid or vertebral atherosclerosis who sustain ischemic stroke to reduce LDL-cholesterol to a level near or below 70 mg/dL.

If treatment with a statin (including trials of higher-dose statins and higher-potency statins) does not achieve the goal selected for a patient, intensifying LDL-lowering drug therapy with an additional drug from among those with evidence of improving outcomes can be effective (bile acid sequestrants or niacin).
For patients who do not tolerate statins, LDL-lowering therapy with bile acid sequestrants and/or niacin is reasonable.

Notes: The relationship between cholesterol and ischemic stroke is not as evident as between cholesterol and myocardial infarction (MI), with inconsistent findings from population-based studies.
The ARIC study found no association of hypercholesterolemia with stroke.

content.General Information: The clinical guideline discusses the relationship between cholesterol levels, statin therapy, and the risk of ischemic stroke in patients. Observational studies highlight that lower HDL cholesterol levels are associated with increased risk of ischemic stroke, whereas statin therapy reduces the risk of stroke in patients with atherosclerosis. Statins also help in lowering the rate of myocardial infarction and cardiovascular death in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD). Various studies and meta-analyses have investigated the effects of statin therapy on cardiovascular health, emphasizing the benefit of lipid-lowering treatments across different populations.

recommendations.dietExerciseDrugs: Useful for patients with diabetes mellitus and extracranial carotid or vertebral artery atherosclerosis.
recommendations.note: The benefit of intensive glucose-lowering therapy to a glycosylated hemoglobin level less than 7.0% has not been established.

recommendations.statinTherapy: Administration of statin-type lipid-lowering medication to reduce LDL cholesterol to near or below 70 mg/dL is reasonable for patients with diabetes mellitus and atherosclerosis.

content.Homocysteine: Various studies explore its relationship with cardiovascular outcomes, including stroke.
content.Vitamin therapy: B-complex vitamin therapy did not confirm a benefit in reducing cardiovascular outcomes, including stroke.
content.Obesity and metabolic syndrome: Associated with carotid atherosclerosis; strongest relationship with hypertension.

content.antiplatelet_agents: Initial treatment with aspirin (50–325 mg/d) alone is recommended, or the combination of aspirin and extended-release dipyridamole, or clopidogrel.
content.combinations: Combination of aspirin and extended-release dipyridamole is recommended over aspirin alone.
content.clopidogrel: May be considered instead of aspirin in patients hypersensitive to aspirin.
content.aspirin_and_clopidogrel: Addition of aspirin to clopidogrel increases the risk of hemorrhage.
clinical_notes: For prevention of thromboembolic ischemic events, antiplatelet drugs reduce risk.
In patients with atherosclerosis of extracranial carotid or vertebral arteries, aspirin is contradindicated by bleeding; clopidogrel is a reasonable alternative.
Full-intensity parenteral anticoagulation not recommended for patients with extracranial cerebrovascular atherosclerosis.
Clopidogrel with aspirin not recommended within 3 months after stroke or TIA.
studies: Asymptomatic Carotid Bruit Study: No benefit demonstrated of platelet-inhibitor drugs for prevention of stroke.
Veterans Affairs Cooperative Study: Some events suggest aspirin may not significantly work in low sample sizes.
ESPS-2 Study: 25 mg aspirin twice daily may be better in selected patients.
PROFESS Study: No significant observations recorded different from previous clopidogrel studies.

content.Optimum therapy for recurrent cerebral i: No known improved outcome with other therapies.

genes.context: Clopidogrel resistance has also been described and its effectiveness is diminished because of genetic variations in the cytochrome P450 system, which depends primarily on the function of CYP2C19. Genetic variability in CYP enzymes that affect platelet function has been associated with adverse outcomes.

guideline_points.point: CEV over CEA is recommended in older patients when arterial pathoanatomy is unfavorable for endovascular intervention.
CEA is recommended over CAS for patients with neck anatomy unfavorable for arterial surgery.
Intervention within 2 weeks is recommended for stroke or TIA patients with no contraindications.
Prophylactic CAS may be considered for selected patients with asymptomatic carotid stenosis, but its effectiveness compared to medical therapy is not well established.
Revascularization effectiveness for patients at high risk due to comorbidities is not well established.
Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) significantly reduces the incidence of ipsilateral stroke.

classifications: Class III: No Benefit - Carotid revascularization is not recommended for disabilities caused by cerebral infarction.

trial_summaries.summary: Designed to test the hypothesis that patients with moderate to severe ipsilateral carotid stenosis would benefit from CEA.
Performed similarly to NASCET, but showed benefit of CEA only for more severe stenosis.

content.overview: This guideline discusses the effectiveness and outcomes of Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA) in asymptomatic patients. It reviews various studies, highlighting differences in treatment groups and the impact of surgical versus medical management. The guideline emphasizes the importance of patient selection based on multiple factors including age, sex, and comorbidities for improved outcomes.

studies.context: The differing methods of measuring stenosis impact the benefit perceived in patients with less severe conditions.

studies.findings: The 30-day risk of stroke or death was 3.1%. Five-year rates, including perioperative events, were 6.4% for early-surgery group vs 11.7% for immediate medical management.
studies.context: Randomized 3,120 asymptomatic patients to compare surgery to medical management with significant findings in long-term outcomes.

After the periprocedural period, the risk of ipsilateral stroke was low and similar in the 2 treatment groups.

Trials.Comments: In both the intention-to-treat and per protocol populations, recurrent stenosis of ≥70% was significantly more frequent in the CAS group than the CEA group, with a life-table estimate of 10.7% versus 4.6% (p=0.0009 and p=0.0007, respectively).

content.comorbidities_and_concerns: Other medical comorbidities contribute to the risk associated with CEA (444, 452, 482). Patients with pulmonary disease, particularly those requiring supplementary oxygen, are at risk of complications including ventilator dependence and pneumonia (483). Renal insufficiency is an independent risk factor for adverse outcomes of pulmonary complications and cardiac events after CEA (484).

content.Circumstances: Patients who undergo radical neck surgery or tracheostomy face surgical challenges due to risks including cranial nerve injury.
content.Radiation Therapy: Designed to avoid severe fibrotic tissue reactions post-surgery. CEA can be performed post-radiation with challenges.
content.Safety of Carotid Surgery: Complication rates for CEA have improved over generations, with lower risks reported recently.
content.Medical Therapy Evolution: Incorporation of pharmacotherapy and surgical advances improved outcomes. Statins and other medications have reduced cardiovascular risks.

Periprocedural Management.Recommendation: Aspirin and clopidogrel or dipyridamole recommended before and after CEA for stroke prophylaxis.

Guideline Recommendations: Administration of antihypertensive medication is recommended as needed to control blood pressure before and after CEA. (Level of Evidence: C)
The findings on clinical neurological examination should be documented within 24 hours before and after CEA. (Level of Evidence: C)
Patch angioplasty can be beneficial for closure of the arteriotomy after CEA. (Level of Evidence: B)

Administration of statin lipid-lowering medication for prevention of ischemic events is reasonable for patients who have undergone CEA irrespective of serum lipid levels, although the optimum agent and dose and the efficacy for prevention of restenosis have not been established (501). (Level of Evidence: B)

Noninvasive imaging of the extracranial carotid arteries is reasonable 1 month, 6 months, and annually after CEA to assess patency and exclude the development of new or contralateral lesions (364,502). Once stability has been established over an extended period, surveillance at longer intervals may be appropriate. Termination of surveillance is reasonable when the patient is no longer a candidate for intervention. (Level of Evidence: C)

ACE (Acetylsalicylic Acid and Carotid En.Findings: Patients assigned to lower-dose aspirin groups had lower rates of perioperative stroke, MI, and death within 30 days. The optimum duration of antithrombotic therapy after CEA is not established, but therapy is reasonable for long-term prevention of ischemic events.

Main Topics.Title: Prevention of Cerebral Embolism in Patients Undergoing Catheter-Based Carotid Intervention
Main Topics.Description: EPDs (Embolic Protection Devices) are effective in reducing embolic material during interventions, particularly in coronary saphenous vein graft angioplasty. They have been shown to reduce rates of ischemic stroke during CAS (Carotid Artery Stenting).

Main Topics.Title: Intravascular Ultrasound Imaging in Conjunction With Catheter-Based Carotid Intervention
Main Topics.Description: IVUS (Intravascular Ultrasound) is an imaging technique used to provide detailed information on arterial diameter and wall. It has been used for coronary and peripheral arteries and may aid in reducing complications like severe plaque protrusion.

Main Topics.Title: Management of Patients Undergoing Endovascular Carotid Artery Stenting
Main Topics.Recommendations: Dual-antiplatelet therapy is recommended before and after CAS. Administration of antihypertensive medications is recommended. EPD deployment during CAS is beneficial for stroke risk reduction.

content.study_design: The SPACE trial was designed to test whether CAS was noninferior to CEA in patients with high-grade carotid artery stenosis. Patients had >70% stenosis and were recruited from 2001-2006. The EVA-3S trial randomized patients within 120 days of TIA or stroke with >60% stenosis. Both trials compared CAS to CEA procedures.
content.outcome_measures: The primary outcome was the composite of ipsilateral stroke or death within 30 days. Secondary outcomes included different types of strokes and disabling strokes.

EVA3S_trial.stopped_early: Enrollment was stopped in 2005 due to higher 30-day rates of stroke and adverse events in CAS.

content.limitations: Unequal training and experience of surgeons performing CAS and non-uniform use of EPDs in CAS procedures.

content.Study Context: The document discusses clinical guidelines for revascularization techniques in patients with carotid artery stenosis. It references studies like CREST which compare carotid artery stenting (CAS) and carotid endarterectomy (CEA) in both symptomatic and asymptomatic patients.
content.Patient Groups: Symptomatic patients with >50% carotid stenosis and asymptomatic patients with >60% stenosis.
content.Key Findings: The 30-day composite rate of stroke, death, or procedural MI was 8.5% in the CAS group versus 5.2% in the CEA group. Primary endpoints in CREST included the occurrence of stroke, death, or MI during the periprocedural period and ipsilateral stroke thereafter up to 4 years.
content.Outcomes: CAS was associated with a higher periprocedural risk of stroke or death but not necessarily with worse long-term outcomes compared to CEA. Age influences outcomes, with CAS favored over CEA for those under 70, and CEA favored for those over 70.
content.Clinical Recommendations: Endarterectomy is generally recommended over stenting for patients with carotid stenosis, especially with factors such as advanced cardiopulmonary disease affecting choice. CAS may have implications for cognitive function due to differences in microembolic cerebral injury.

Recommendation: In patients with symptomatic cerebral ischemia and recurrent carotid stenosis due to intimal hyperplasia or atherosclerosis, reoperation through CEA or CAS is recommended using the same criteria as for initial revascularization.
Reoperative CEA or CAS after initial revascularization is reasonable when imaging identifies rapidly progressive restenosis indicating a complete occlusion.

In asymptomatic patients with recurrent carotid stenosis due to intimal hyperplasia or atherosclerosis, reoperative CEA or CAS may be considered using the established criteria for initial revascularization.
Reoperative CEA or CAS should not be performed in asymptomatic patients with less than 70% carotid stenosis that has remained stable.

Clinical Durability: Clinical durability refers to the sustained efficacy of CEA and CAS in preventing stroke.
In larger trials, ipsilateral stroke rates 30 days post-procedure were about 1% to 2% for symptomatic and 0.5% to 0.8% for asymptomatic patients.
Clinical durability for CEA and CAS beyond 5 years is not clearly determined.
Primary causes of early restenosis are usually intimal hyperplasia, while late restenosis typically involves progression of atherosclerosis.
Anatomic Durability: The incidence of recurrent carotid stenosis depends on the detection method, with lower rates when patches are used.
Recurrent carotid stenosis after CEA is usually a tri-modal phenomenon.
For both CEA and CAS, stenosis is rare after proper procedures.

Topics: Symptomatic obstructive disease of the vertebral arteries is less common than carotid stenosis in clinical practice.
The prevalence, pathophysiology, and natural history of vertebral artery disease are not well understood.
Patients with vertebral artery disease face increased risks of other cardiovascular ischemic events like MI, PAD, and vascular death.

8.1. Anatomy of the Vertebrobasilar Arte: The left and right vertebral arteries are split into 4 segments (V1 through V4).
Anatomical variants are more common than in the carotid circulation.
Significant portions of the population may have variations affecting blood flow.

8.2. Epidemiology of Vertebral Artery Di: Approx. 20% of posterior circulation strokes may be due to vertebral artery atherosclerosis.
The New England Medical Center Posterior Circulation Registry found significant stenosis association with higher stroke risks.
Studies indicate patients with combined carotid and vertebral artery stenosis face greater ischemic episodes.

content.Clinical Presentation: Atherosclerotic stenosis affects the vertebral arteries, causing symptoms like dizziness, blurred vision, and syncope due to artery stenosis or occlusive disease.
content.Evaluation of Patients: Noninvasive imaging like CTA or MRA is recommended for evaluating vertebral artery disease.
content.Imaging Recommendations: Recommendations include using CTA or MRA over ultrasound for vertebral artery evaluation, and catheter-based angiography when necessary.
content.Medical Therapy: Lifestyle modifications and medications like aspirin or clopidogrel are recommended for managing atherosclerosis involving the vertebral arteries.

content.Optimum management of patients with symp: Vertebral artery reconstruction has perioperative mortality rates of 0% to 4%, with more complex cases showing higher risks. Outcomes of angioplasty and stenting are comparable for carotid artery disease. Despite technical feasibility, such interventions show varied results and lack strong evidence for preventing complications greater than conservative management.

content.Title: Diseases of the Subclavian and Brachiocephalic Arteries

Sections.Title: 9.1. Recommendations for the Management of Patients With Occlusive Disease of the Subclavian and Brachiocephalic Arteries
Class IIa: Extra-anatomic carotid-subclavian bypass is reasonable for patients with symptomatic posterior cerebral or cerebellar ischemia caused by subclavian artery stenosis or occlusion, in the absence of clinical factors predisposing to surgical morbidity or mortality.

Percutaneous endovascular angioplasty and stenting is reasonable for patients with symptomatic posterior cerebral or cerebellar ischemia caused by subclavian artery stenosis who are at high risk of surgical complications.

Revascularization by percutaneous angioplasty and stenting, direct arterial reconstruction, or extra-anatomic bypass surgery is reasonable for patients with symptomatic ischemia involving the anterior cerebral circulation caused by common carotid or brachiocephalic artery occlusive disease.

Revascularization by percutaneous angioplasty and stenting, direct arterial reconstruction, or extra-anatomic bypass surgery is reasonable for patients with symptomatic ischemia involving upper extremity claudication caused by subclavian or brachiocephalic arterial occlusive disease.

Revascularization by either extra-anatomic bypass surgery or subclavian angioplasty and stenting is reasonable for asymptomatic patients with subclavian artery stenosis when the ipsilateral internal mammary artery is required as a conduit for myocardial revascularization.
Class III: No Benefit: Asymptomatic patients with asymmetrical upper-limb blood pressure, periclavicular bruit, or flow reversal in a vertebral artery caused by subclavian artery stenosis should not undergo revascularization unless the internal mammary artery is required for myocardial revascularization.

Sections.Title: 9.2. Occlusive Disease of the Subclavian and Brachiocephalic Arteries
Sections.Description: Occlusive disease of these arteries is relatively uncommon and caused by atherosclerosis, Takayasu arteritis, giant cell arteritis, FMD, and radiation-induced arteriopathy. Symptoms may include upper-extremity ischemia, claudication, paresthesia, or pain.

Sections.Title: 9.3. Subclavian Steal Syndrome
Sections.Description: When the proximal subclavian artery becomes stenotic or occluded, collateral circulation may cause muscle fatigue in the affected arm or claudication. Symptoms may be aggravated by exercising the ipsilateral arm. Diagnosis includes identifying flow reversal in a vertebral artery.

content.section: 9.4. Revascularization of the Brachiocephalic and Subclavian Arteries
approaches: Prosthetic extra-anatomic bypass grafting from the ipsilateral carotid artery to the subclavian artery is used to restore blood flow.
Extra-anatomic methods include carotid-axillary or axilloaxillary bypass with polytetrafluoroethylene or Dacron grafts.
Surgical repair associated with low morbidity and high long-term patency.
Subclavian artery stenosis can be treated with angioplasty, atherectomy, and stenting.
Balloon angioplasty and stenting have better outcomes compared to angioplasty alone.
Some reports favor bypass surgery for good surgical candidates.
Brachiocephalic artery occlusive disease is often treated by carotid or subclavian artery stenosis, with transthoracic revascularization preferred in embolic cases.
procedure success rates: Procedure was initially successful in 93% of cases in one study.
High 1-year and 5-year patency rates: 100% at 1 year, 96% at 5 years post-surgical bypass.
risks: Complications can include embolism, heart failure, and arterial pseudoneurysm.
Balloon angioplasty and stenting showed fewer complications compared to bypass surgery.
recommendations: Angioplasty and stenting recommended for subclavian and brachiocephalic arteries.
Bypass surgery is preferred for certain candidates, percutaneous stenting when anatomy permits.

sections.title: 10.1. Neurological Risk Reduction in Patients With Carotid Artery Disease Undergoing Cardiac or Noncardiac Surgery

subsections.title: 10.1.1 Recommendations for Carotid Artery Evaluation and Revascularization Before Cardiac Surgery

recommendations: Carotid duplex ultrasound screening is reasonable before elective CABG surgery in patients older than 65 years of age and in those with left main coronary stenosis, PAD, a history of cigarette smoking, a history of stroke or TIA, or carotid bruit. (Level of Evidence: C)

Carotid revascularization by CEA or CAS with embolic protection before or concurrent with myocardial revascularization surgery is reasonable in patients with greater than 80% carotid stenosis who have experienced ipsilateral retinal or hemispheric cerebral ischemic symptoms within 6 months. (Level of Evidence: C)

In patients with asymptomatic carotid stenosis, even if severe, the safety and efficacy of carotid revascularization before or concurrent with myocardial revascularization are not well established. (Level of Evidence: C)

subsections.title: 10.1.2 Neurological Risk Reduction in Patients With Carotid Artery Disease Undergoing Coronary Bypass Surgery
subsections.summary: CABG patients without carotid atherosclerosis face higher stroke risks than patients without carotid disease. Evidence is inconclusive on the benefit of carotid intervention in asymptomatic cases. With symptomatic carotid stenosis patients, there are indications that certain approaches, like performing CEA before CABG, may reduce the risk of stroke and death.

subsections.title: 10.1.3 Neurological Risk Reduction in Patients Undergoing Noncoronary Cardiac or Noncardiac Surgery
subsections.summary: For professional guidelines on managing such patients, the 2009 ACCF/AHA focused update is recommended.

sections.title: 11. Nonatherosclerotic Carotid and Vertebral Artery Diseases
sections.summary: Nonatherosclerotic diseases of these arteries, such as FMD and arterial dissection, are uncommon and less understood compared to atherosclerosis.

Class IIa: Annual noninvasive imaging of the carotid arteries is reasonable initially for patients with FMD to detect changes in the extent or severity of disease.
Administration of platelet-inhibitor medication can be beneficial in patients with FMD of the carotid arteries to prevent thromboembolism.
Carotid angioplasty with or without stenting is reasonable for patients with retinal or hemispheric cerebral ischemic symptoms related to FMD of the ipsilateral carotid artery.
Class III: No Benefit: Revascularization is not recommended for patients with asymptomatic FMD of a carotid artery, regardless of the severity of stenosis.
11.1.1. Recommendations for Management o.General Information: FMD is a nonatherosclerotic, noninflammatory vascular disease characterized by focal stenosis or multiple constrictions. The incidence of carotid FMD is low and is most commonly encountered in middle-aged women. Symptoms can include stroke, TIA, carotid dissection, Horner syndrome, cranial nerve palsies, and subarachnoid hemorrhage.

Class I: Contrast-enhanced CTA, MRA, and catheter-based contrast angiography are useful for the diagnosis of cervical artery dissection.
Class IIa: Antithrombotic treatment with either an anticoagulant or a platelet inhibitor for at least 3 to 6 months is reasonable for patients.
Class IIb: Carotid angioplasty and stenting might be considered when ischemic neurological symptoms have not responded to antithrombotic therapy.

The safety and effectiveness of pharmacological therapy with a beta-adrenergic antagonist, angiotensin inhibitor, or nonhydropyridine calcium channel antagonist to lower blood pressure and reduce arterial wall stress are not well established.
11.2.1. Recommendations for Management o.General Information: Carotid or vertebral artery dissection can occur spontaneously or after minor trauma. Symptoms like hyperflexion, hyperextension of the neck, or chiropractic manipulation have been associated with dissection. Dissection can stem from an intimal tear leading to intramural hematomas.

Risk Factors: Cephalad motion of the vertebral artery
Symptoms: Pain on one side of the head or neck
Treatment: Anticoagulation with heparin followed by warfarin

Understand the connection between carotid stenosis and ischemic brain events

content.Summary: The document discusses advancements in imaging technologies for evaluating patients with Extracranial Cerebrovascular Disease (ECVD). It highlights the limitations of current methods and the need for improved imaging to identify stroke risk. The use of antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation in patients with ECVD and those who suffer acute ischemic stroke is covered, alongside the importance of investigating drug resistance and genetic profiling. The paper calls for clinical guidelines to focus on individual patient evaluations based on risk factors and personalized medicine approaches.
content.Clinical Imaging: PET/CT, PET/MRI are used to evaluate ECVD by identifying plaque instability and inflammation biomarkers. MRI and CT are expanding into wider patient demographics.
content.Antiplatelet Therapy: Current treatments are not fully proven effective for asymptomatic patients with ECVD. Ongoing research is needed to find optimal antithrombotic therapy.
content.Drug Resistance: Emphasizes the need for genetic profiling to understand drug resistance in treatment.
content.Anticoagulation: Few studies are available on its use post-acute ischemic stroke in ECVD patients. Increased risk of ischemic injury poses challenges.
content.Specific Therapies: Antihypertensive therapy is under evaluation for those with impaired cerebral perfusion. Importance of targeting cholesterol and ischemic stroke prevention strategies.
content.Gender and Demography: Acknowledges the need to address differences in stroke presentation and treatment outcomes related to sex, ethnicity, and other clinical features.

content.general_information: The clinical guideline discusses careful consideration for the duration and intensity of antithrombotic therapy after revascularization, noting differences in treatment efficacy among demographic groups like women, older patients, and middle-aged white males. It emphasizes the need for more research on vascular lumen, pathobiology of atherosclerosis, plaque composition, and calcification to explore clinical manifestations of ECVD. CREST results provide guidance on carotid artery stenting and endarterectomy, urging against extrapolation of current practice until more structured trials are conducted. It underlines the complexity of vertebral arterial disease due to its rarity compared to carotid stenosis.
clinical_points: Attention to the long-term prevention of ischemic events in patients after revascularization.
Need for research on differences in outcomes based on demographic variables.
CREST trial results indicate comparable outcomes for CAS and CEA but emphasize further studies for older patients.
Consideration of patient-specific factors in treatment strategies for symptomatic and asymptomatic patients.
Challenges in studying less common ECVD forms even in controlled trials.

Meta-analyses and reviews on the risk-benefit profiles of carotid surgeries and medications in stroke prevention.

topics: Safety and efficacy of carotid stenting
highlights: Carotid artery stenting is evaluated in various clinical trials and patient follow-ups.
Embolic protection devices are essential for protecting against complications during stenting.
Stent restenosis and in-stent neointimal proliferation are challenging issues in patients.
Balloon angioplasty with stenting has shown varying results in different trials.
Hyperperfusion syndrome should be distinguished carefully post-stenting.
